flashcards.front: What is the primary goal of memory management strategies in operating systems?
flashcards.back: To efficiently allocate and deallocate main memory (RAM) among multiple processes, maximizing utilization while ensuring protection and isolation between processes.

flashcards.front: What is swapping in memory management?
flashcards.back: Swapping is the process of moving entire processes between main memory and secondary storage (disk). It allows the OS to temporarily remove inactive processes from RAM to make space for other processes.
A memory management technique where each process is allocated a single continuous block of physical memory. The entire process must fit in one contiguous segment.

flashcards.front: What is paging? How does it differ from contiguous allocation?
flashcards.back: Paging divides both memory and logical memory physical into fixed-size blocks called frames and pages respectively. Unlike contiguous allocation, a process can be scattered across non-contiguous physical frames.

A data structure maintained by the OS that maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers. It contains information about each page including its validity, protection bits, and location in memory or disk.

Segmentation divides a process's logical address space into variable-sized segments (code, data, stack, heap). Each segment has a name and length, providing logical organization and protection at segment level.
A technique that allows execution of processes that may not be completely in main memory. It creates an illusion of a larger memory than physically available using paging and demand loading.
A virtual memory management technique where pages are loaded into memory only when they are needed (upon a page fault). Pages not currently needed remain in secondary storage.
A memory optimization technique where fork() creates child processes with shared pages. Pages are copied only when either process tries to modify them, saving memory and copy time.

flashcards.front: What are common page replacement algorithms?
flashcards.back: FIFO (First-In-First-Out), LRU (Least Recently Used), Optimal (OPT), and Second Chance (Clock). These algorithms determine which page to evict when a page fault occurs and memory is full.

Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time swapping pages in and out than executing processes. It happens when there are too many processes competing for too few frames, causing excessive page faults.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model in memory management?
flashcards.back: The working set is the set of pages a process has referenced in the recent past. The OS ensures each process has enough frames to hold its working set to minimize thrashing.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation?
flashcards.back: Internal fragmentation occurs when allocated memory is larger than requested (wasted space within allocated block). External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small blocks, unable to satisfy large requests.

flashcards.front: What is a translation lookaside buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: TLB is a special hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations. It speeds up memory access by avoiding page table lookups for frequently used pages.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of frame allocation algorithms?
flashcards.back: Frame allocation algorithms determine how to distribute available physical frames among processes. Examples include equal allocation, proportional allocation, and priority-based allocation.
